title: Text_analysis
Text_analysis
Text_analysis
Text_analysis
text:
Tweeting time
As someone who treats x as her newspaper, that means I spend my mornings and evenings scrolling and sometimes tweeting. I want to see what time of the day the ex presidential couple usually tweeted.

tweets_tidy |>
ggplot(aes(x = hour,y= person, fill = person)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
  title = "Tweeting Time Distribution by Person",
  x = "Hour of Day",
  y = "Person"
)








This is a horizontal box plot comparing the tweeting time Distribution of President Obama and Michelle. The box of each person has a different color, President Obama’s is coral and Michelle’s is teal.The x-axis represents the hour of day from 0 to 24, and the y-axis shows the person. Both individuals show high activity in afternoons and evenings. Their median tweet times around hour 5:00 Pm. President Barack’s distribution is slightly more condensed between hours 4pm and 8pm, while Michelle’s box is wider, ranging roughly from 2pm to 8pm. Both of them have several outlier points in the early morning hours between midnight and 6 am.

title: Text_analysis
Text_analysis
Text_analysis
text:
Conclusion
This analysis compared the Twitter activity of President Barack Obama and Michelle Obama during the final years of President Obama’s presidency.The analysis revealed that both individuals tended to tweet during the same time and that Michelle Obama tweeted more frequently. Word frequency analysis and sentiment analysis revealed common optimistic language but differences in thematic focus. President Obama’s tweets often referenced national issues and civic engagement, while Michelle Obama’s tweets emphasized education and youth initiatives.

title: About
text: This website is resource I will be using to share my work

title: Maps
text:
library(sf)
states <- read_sf("https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/json/us-states.geojson") 

library(leaflet)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyverse)


The states dataset in the poliscidata package contains 135 variables on each of the 50 US states. See here for more detail.

Your task is to create a two meaningful choropleth plots, one using a numeric variable and one using a categorical variable from poliscidata::states. You should make two versions of each plot: a static plot using the maps package and ggplot(), and an interactive plot using the sf package and leaflet(). Write a sentence or two describing what you can learn from each plot.
Here’s some R code and hints to get you going:

polisci_hw4 <- poliscidata::states |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(states = str_to_lower(str_squish(as.character(state)))) |>
  select(states, college, cook_index3)

states_poly_hw4 <- as_tibble(map_data("state")) |>
  mutate(states = str_to_lower(region)) |>
  left_join(polisci_hw4, by = "states")

states_sf_clean <- read_sf("https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/json/us-states.geojson") |>
  mutate(state_key = str_to_lower(name),
         states = state_key) |>
  filter(!name %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii", "Puerto Rico")) |>
  left_join(polisci_hw4, by = "states")

static numeric variable :

ggplot(states_poly_hw4,
       aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, fill = college)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_map("polyconic") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c( name = "college index") +
  labs(title = "percentage of people who attend college")

from this map we can learn that majority of states have between 20% and 35% college attendance
interactive numeric variable :

num_pal <- colorNumeric("YlOrRd", domain = states_sf_clean$college)

leaflet(states_sf_clean) |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~num_pal(college),
    weight = 1, color = "white", 
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    label = ~paste0(name, college),
   highlightOptions = highlightOptions(   
      weight = 3, 
      color = "black", 
      bringToFront = TRUE
    )
  )

This map is very helpful because as a non-American who is not familiar with state names, I get to know the state name and see the percentage college attendance as I interact with the map
static categorical varivale

ggplot(states_poly_hw4, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, fill = cook_index3)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_map("polyconic") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Dem" = "blue", "Even" = "purple", "Rep" = "red"), 
                    name = "Partisanship") +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "US States Cook partisan index")

from this map, we can see that majority of the US states do not have a prefered cook party
intercative categorical variable

cat_pal <- colorFactor( palette = c("blue", "purple", "red"), 
  domain = states_sf_clean$cook_index3
)

leaflet(states_sf_clean) |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~cat_pal(cook_index3),
    weight = 1, color = "white", fillOpacity = 0.7,
    label = ~paste0(name, ": ", cook_index3),
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(weight = 2, color = "#666", bringToFront = TRUE)
  ) |>
  addLegend(pal = cat_pal, values = ~cook_index3, title = "Political Lean")

from this graph we get to interact with the plot and see where the people actually lean towards in term of political party
